disclaimer: Market data and narratives are for informational and educational use only and are not investment advice. Figures are sourced from publicly available market data and news reports available at refresh time; verify prices before making decisions.
marketPulse: Risk-on tape shrugs off oil shock as earnings carry the close
marketPulseSubtext: U.S. equities finished sharply higher, with SPY +1.0%, QQQ +0.9%, DIA +1.6%, IWM +2.2%, and SOXX +2.6%. Oil remained the macro shock variable, but a late pullback in Brent and WTI let earnings strength, breadth, and AI/cloud optimism dominate the close.
topDriver: Oil shock fading intraday while earnings and AI capex optimism supported risk
narrativeSummary: April ended with a classic wall-of-worry rally: stocks pushed to record levels even as crude remained historically elevated because the immediate oil spike faded into the close. The dominant market read was not that geopolitical risk disappeared, but that the tape could absorb it as long as oil stopped accelerating and earnings kept beating. Strong GDP, low jobless claims, and firm PCE inflation left the Fed in a higher-for-longer posture, while the Treasury curve stayed steep with long-end yields near 5%. The AI complex was mixed beneath the surface: Alphabet, Amazon, semis, and data-center beneficiaries helped risk appetite, while Meta and Microsoft reminded investors that capex discipline still matters. The setup remains constructive but fragile: equities are celebrating earnings breadth, while oil, inflation, and long-duration rates still define the downside tail.
macroStressSubtext: Oil and long rates still elevated, but equity breadth and lower VIX eased stress.
rateCurveRead: The Treasury curve remained steep, with short bills clustered near 3.7% while the 10Y held around 4.40% and the 30Y near 4.98%. That shape keeps the market in an inflation-risk regime: growth is still holding up, but long-end duration is demanding compensation for energy shock and policy uncertainty.

scenarios.label: Bear
scenarios.title: Oil shock re-accelerates
scenarios.trigger: Brent reclaims the $120-$126 zone while Hormuz disruption persists and long yields rise again.
scenarios.outcome: Equity multiples compress, VIX lifts back above 20, energy and defensives outperform, and rate-sensitive growth fades.

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.title: Earnings absorb the macro shock
scenarios.trigger: Oil remains elevated but stops making new highs while earnings revisions and breadth keep improving.
scenarios.outcome: Indexes grind higher with rotation into cyclicals, semis, health care, and industrials, but leadership stays sensitive to AI capex headlines.
watchpoints: Equal-weight S&P keeps pace with SPY

scenarios.label: Bull
scenarios.title: Oil risk premium unwinds
scenarios.trigger: Diplomatic progress or shipping normalization pushes crude lower while inflation fears ease.
scenarios.outcome: Rates stabilize, VIX compresses, small caps and cyclicals lead, and the AI-capex trade regains cleaner sponsorship.

flows.note: Broad index gains and strong advancers suggested money moved back into risk after the oil spike cooled. Small caps outperformed, signaling more than mega-cap-only sponsorship.
SOXX outperformed as investors rewarded AI infrastructure demand, Qualcomm hyperscaler news, and strong cloud growth despite capex concerns.
TLT was slightly lower while the 30Y yield stayed near 4.98%, consistent with limited appetite for duration amid oil-driven inflation risk.
Crude futures fell from extreme intraday levels, but energy risk premium remained high because Hormuz disruption is unresolved.
Gold rose even as equities rallied, indicating investors still wanted geopolitical and inflation hedges.
The dollar weakened sharply as yen intervention headlines hit FX, reducing one source of global tightening pressure.

flowsRead: The flow picture was not pure risk-on; it was selective risk-on with hedges still attached. Equities, small caps, semis, and health care caught sponsorship, but gold held a haven bid and duration failed to rally meaningfully. That combination keeps the SPI elevated: growth-sensitive assets are participating, yet energy inflation and long-rate pressure remain the macro tax. The AICS read improved because cloud demand and AI infrastructure revenue signals offset investor anxiety about capex intensity.

topThemes.theme: Oil risk premium cools but remains the macro tax
topThemes.impact: Crude fell from shock highs, allowing equities to rally, but Brent above $110 keeps inflation and policy risk alive.
Strong results from Alphabet, Amazon, Caterpillar, Eli Lilly, and chip-linked names supported a broad month-end bid.

topThemes.theme: AI capex trade splits winners from spenders
topThemes.impact: Cloud growth and semiconductor demand were rewarded, while investors punished companies where capex pressure looked too large.

topThemes.theme: Steep curve warns inflation is not solved
topThemes.impact: The front end was contained, but 10Y and 30Y yields stayed elevated, signaling a higher-for-longer regime.
VIX cooled toward the high teens despite oil and geopolitical risk, creating a fragile calm rather than an all-clear.

sectorHeat.driver: SOXX +2.6% as chip and hyperscaler demand stayed central to the earnings tape.
XLV +2.2%, helped by Eli Lilly strength and renewed interest in quality growth.
Caterpillar and infrastructure-linked names supported Dow leadership.
XLY +1.3% as lower intraday oil pressure helped the consumer read.
XLF +0.4%, with the steep curve supportive but macro uncertainty limiting enthusiasm.
XLK barely higher as cloud winners competed with capex-driven weakness in select mega-cap names.
Crude pulled back from extreme highs, but sector risk remains tied to Hormuz and Brent volatility.

drivers.name: Oil shock reversal from intraday extremes
drivers.detail: Brent touched $126.41 before settling at $114.01, letting equities rally while keeping inflation risk elevated.

drivers.name: Broad equity rally into month-end
drivers.detail: The S&P 500, Dow, Nasdaq, and Russell 2000 all advanced, with small caps and cyclicals showing participation beyond mega-cap tech.
drivers.source: AP index close; Reuters Wall Street wrap

drivers.name: AI/cloud earnings support
drivers.detail: Alphabet cloud strength, AWS growth, and semiconductor-related optimism reinforced the AI infrastructure narrative, even as capex scrutiny stayed high.
drivers.source: Reuters Alphabet; AP Amazon; Barron's Microsoft

drivers.name: Firm macro data keeps Fed cautious
drivers.detail: Q1 GDP grew 2.0%, PCE inflation rose 3.5% year over year, and jobless claims fell to 189,000, reducing near-term cut pressure.

drivers.name: Long-end yields stay near stress levels
drivers.detail: The 10Y closed near 4.40% and the 30Y near 4.98%, keeping valuation and housing pressure in the background.
drivers.source: U.S. Treasury daily par yield curve

drivers.name: Volatility compression
drivers.detail: VIX moved lower toward the high teens, signaling less immediate hedging demand despite unresolved geopolitical risk.

risks.title: Hormuz disruption re-prices oil higher
risks.desc: Any renewed blockade escalation or military action could push Brent back toward the $120-$126 shock range and reignite inflation stress.

risks.title: Long-end yield pressure breaks equity multiples
risks.desc: The 30Y near 5% remains a valuation headwind, especially for duration-heavy growth and housing-sensitive sectors.

risks.title: AI capex skepticism spreads
risks.desc: If investors shift from rewarding cloud growth to punishing capex intensity, mega-cap tech leadership could narrow quickly.

risks.title: Breadth divergence under the surface
risks.desc: Indexes are strong, but technical breadth warnings suggest the rally still needs continued participation from equal-weight, small caps, and cyclicals.

risks.title: Inflation data keeps Fed boxed in
risks.desc: PCE above target plus energy pass-through lowers the odds of a near-term easing pivot.

timeline.headline: Oil spikes again on Iran/Hormuz fears
timeline.effect: Brent briefly traded above $126, putting stagflation and supply shock risk back at the center of the tape.

timeline.headline: Economic data shows growth plus inflation
timeline.effect: GDP rebounded, PCE stayed hot, and jobless claims surprised lower, supporting a higher-for-longer Fed read.

timeline.headline: Crude reverses and equities broaden
timeline.effect: Oil pulled back from shock highs, small caps joined the rally, and volatility compressed.

timeline.headline: Earnings leadership takes over
timeline.effect: Alphabet, Amazon, Caterpillar, Eli Lilly, and chip-linked names supported risk appetite into the close.

timeline.headline: Stocks finish strong despite unresolved macro shock
timeline.effect: SPY, QQQ, DIA, IWM, and SOXX closed higher while gold and long yields showed hedges remained in place.

timeline.headline: Can oil stay below the panic zone?
timeline.effect: A sustained Brent move back above $120 would likely flip the tape from earnings-led risk-on back to stagflation stress.

watchpoints.event: Oil shipping and Hormuz traffic headlines
Energy futures curve and Brent contract rollover effects
Mega-cap earnings digestion and AI capex commentary
Treasury yield curve response to inflation pass-through
VIX and volatility futures term structure
AMD, Disney, Palantir, PayPal, and ad-tech earnings watch

strategies.title: Favor broadening over mega-cap-only beta
strategies.body: The strongest message today was not simply tech strength; it was small-cap and cyclical participation alongside semis. Broadening leadership is healthier than another narrow AI-only chase.
strategies.trigger: IWM and RSP continue outperforming SPY for multiple sessions.

strategies.title: Keep oil shock hedges active
strategies.body: Equities can rally while oil falls from extremes, but Brent above $110 is still a macro tax. Energy exposure, inflation hedges, or tactical downside protection remain relevant until Hormuz risk fades.

strategies.title: Separate AI revenue winners from capex prisoners
strategies.body: The AI trade is becoming more discriminating. Prefer companies converting cloud or infrastructure demand into visible revenue and margin support, while avoiding names where capex spend overwhelms the story.
strategies.trigger: Cloud growth and backlog beat capex growth in earnings commentary.

strategies.title: Watch duration as the market’s hidden veto
strategies.body: The 30Y near 5% can cap multiples even when earnings are strong. A clean equity breakout is easier if the 10Y/30Y stop rising.

strategies.title: Use VIX compression carefully
strategies.body: Lower VIX supports risk appetite, but geopolitics and oil create gap risk. Volatility dips may be better used for hedging than complacency.
strategies.trigger: VIX below 17 while Brent stays above $110.
